title: Air Fryer Mozzarella Sticks
instructions: Cut the cheese sticks in half, crosswise. Set aside.

Whisk together the eggs and water in a small bowl. In another small bowl, combine the breadcrumbs, panko, Italian seasoning, garlic powder, and paprika, stirring well. In a third bowl, combine the flour, salt, and pepper, stirring well.
Dip each of the cheese sticks into the eggs, then dredge in the flour mixture. Dip back into the eggs, then dredge in the bread crumb mixture, pressing to coat well.

Place the sticks on a small parchment-lined baking sheet. Freeze for 30 minutes. After 30 minutes, dip each of the sticks back into the eggs, then dredge in the breadcrumb mixture, pressing to coat well. Freeze again for 30 minutes.

Preheat the air fryer to 390°, if required. Spray the fryer basket with cooking spray. Generously coat the frozen sticks with cooking spray. In 2 batches, place the sticks in the basket in a single layer. Cook 6-7 minutes or until browned and crispy. (Keep an eye on them so they do not overcook or the cheese will start to bubble out.)
Serve immediately with warm marinara sauce.
notes: A double dredge in breadcrumb coating ensures crunchy results. Prep time: 20 min | Servings: 4–6

title: Biscuits & Sausage Gravy
instructions: Preheat oven to 425°.
Brown sausage in a non-stick skillet. Set aside. Do not drain off grease.

Mix flour, salt, sugar, baking powder in a bowl. Add in cubed butter and squeeze butter into the flour with your hands until the consistency is like corn meal and there are no big clumps of butter in the mix.
Add milk and stir. This makes a slightly sticky dough.
Sprinkle a little flour on the counter. Turn dough out of the bowl on to the counter and sprinkle top of dough with a light dusting of flour.
Using a rolling pin, gently roll out to about 1/2 inch thick and cut 9 biscuits.
Place biscuits in cold cast iron skillet. Put in the oven and bake 12 minutes or until golden brown on top.

To make gravy, put the skillet with sausage back on the heat. Stir 2 Tbsp flour into the sausage. You may need to add a little butter. Stir until flour is mixed well with the sausage and flour has browned slightly and there are no lumps of flour.
Pour in half the milk and bring to a low boil — this will start to thicken really fast.
Add Kitchen Bouquet to brown up the color of the gravy. Slowly keep adding milk until it is all stirred in and thickened.
Biscuits should be ready about the same time the gravy is finished. Add salt and pepper to taste.
notes: Sharon's recipe.

title: Canning White Potatoes
ingredients: Metal skewer for de-bubbling, ladle, wide mouth funnel, jar lifter
instructions: 8 AM: Wash and peel potatoes. Dice into 1-2 inch chunks and soak covered with water.
Noon: Rinse potatoes and cover with water again.
4 PM: Rinse potatoes and cover with water again.
8 PM: Rinse potatoes and cover with water again. Let soak overnight.
Rinse potatoes. Add water to stock pot and bring to a boil. Add two quarts of potatoes and cook 2 minutes.
While potatoes are coming up to temperature, put canner on stove. Ensure canning rack is in the bottom. Add 3 quarts of water and 2 Tbsp white vinegar to the canner (keeps jars from clouding).
Fill a large sauce pan with water and bring to a boil (for packing the potatoes). Add 1 tsp salt to each of the 7 jars.

As timer finishes on potatoes, pack hot potatoes into jars. Fill jar with fresh hot water and de-bubble. Add more water if needed, leaving no more than 1 inch headspace. Wipe the rim of the jar with a paper towel dipped in vinegar. Add lid and ring, finger tighten and place in the canner. Repeat until all potatoes are packed.
Put lid on canner, heat on high until a steady stream of steam comes from the vent pipe. Vent steam for 10 minutes. Then put pressure regulator on the vent pipe.

Continue heating until pressure dial gauge registers the correct pressure (11 lbs). Adjust heat to maintain correct pressure for the duration of processing time (40 minutes for quarts). If pressure falls below 11 lbs, restart the processing time.
At the end of processing time, turn off heat and remove canner from the eye. Let the canner cool naturally. Do NOT remove the regulator.

Once canner has cooled and all pressure has been released and pressure valves have dropped, open canner and remove jars one at a time. Place on a dry towel with space between jars. Listen for the pings. Let stand overnight to cool.
Remove rings and check seals. Wash jars (do not store with rings on). Wash rings. Store in a dark, cool, dry place.
notes: STL Altitude 590 ft. Hot Pack method. Pints: 35 minutes @ 11 lbs of pressure. Quarts: 40 minutes @ 11 lbs of pressure. My canner will only hold 7 quart jars. Note: Directions say 20 lbs of potatoes yields 7 quarts — my experience is that 10 lbs yields 7 quarts.

In a medium heat-safe bowl, whisk together 1 cup of milk, egg yolks, salt, and cornstarch until smooth and well-combined.
While whisking the egg mixture, slowly pour 1.5 cups of the hot milk mixture into the bowl to temper the eggs.
Return the milk pot to the stove over medium heat. Slowly pour the tempered egg mixture into the milk pot while whisking the whole time.
Whisk pudding constantly until it returns to a boil. Immediately reduce heat to a simmer and continue to whisk until the pudding is thick, about 2–3 minutes more.
Remove from heat and stir in vanilla and butter until fully incorporated. If not smooth, strain through a fine-mesh sieve.
Place pudding into a container and lay plastic wrap directly on the pudding surface to prevent a skin from forming. Chill until set, 4 hours to overnight. Serve with whipped cream.

title: Crisp-Skinned Spatchcocked Roast Turkey With Gravy
filename: Crisp-Skinned Spatchcocked Roast Turkey With Gravy.html
ingredients: 3 large carrots, peeled and roughly chopped (about 1 quart)
1 whole turkey (12–14 lbs total), butterflied/spatchcocked, backbone, neck, and giblets reserved
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
1 1/2 quarts low-sodium chicken or turkey broth
instructions: Adjust oven rack to middle position and preheat oven to 450°F (230°C). Line a rimmed baking sheet or broiler pan with aluminum foil. Scatter 2/3 of the onions, carrots, celery, and thyme across the bottom. Place a slotted broiler rack or wire rack directly on top of vegetables.

Pat turkey dry with paper towels and rub on all surfaces with 1 tbsp oil. Season liberally with salt and black pepper. Tuck wing tips behind back. Place turkey on rack, pressing down on the breast bone to flatten slightly.
Roast, rotating occasionally, until breast registers 150°F (66°C) and thighs register at least 165°F (74°C), about 80 minutes.

While turkey roasts, make the gravy: Roughly chop the neck, backbone, and giblets. Heat remaining 1 tbsp oil in a 3-quart saucepan over high heat. Add chopped turkey parts and cook, stirring, until lightly browned, about 5 minutes.

Add remaining onions, carrots, and celery. Cook, stirring occasionally, until softened and browned in spots, 5 minutes. Add broth, remaining thyme, and bay leaves. Bring to a boil, reduce to a bare simmer, and cook 45 minutes. Strain through a fine-mesh strainer; skim fat.

Melt butter over medium-high heat in a 2-quart saucepan. Add flour and cook, stirring constantly, until golden brown, about 3 minutes. Whisking constantly, add strained broth in a thin steady stream. Bring to a boil, reduce to a simmer, and cook until reduced to about 1 quart, about 20 minutes. Season with salt and pepper; keep warm.
Remove turkey from oven and transfer rack to a new baking sheet. Rest 20 minutes before carving. Whisk any collected juices into gravy.
Carve turkey and serve with gravy.
notes: Dry brine (recommended): Combine 1/2 cup Diamond Crystal kosher salt (or 6 tbsp Morton's kosher salt) with 2 tbsp baking powder. Pat turkey dry. Generously sprinkle mixture on all surfaces. Transfer to a rack on a rimmed baking sheet and refrigerate uncovered for 12–24 hours. (Up to 3 days is even better — cover loosely after 24 hours to prevent excess moisture loss.) Roast without rinsing; omit additional salt in the recipe.

title: Dehydrated Potatoes
ingredients: Potatoes (waxy varieties like red or Yukon Gold recommended; russets also work)
instructions: Peel the potatoes and cut off any bad spots.
Soak peeled potatoes in water to prevent browning and remove some starch.
Slice, dice (no more than 1/3" thick), or shred the potatoes.

Bring a large pot of water to a full rolling boil. Add potatoes and blanch until fork tender — about 1–2 minutes for thin slices, up to 5 minutes for small cubes. Do not over-cook; they should pierce easily but not be mushy.
Drain and rinse potatoes with cold water to stop cooking.
Spread potatoes in a single layer on dehydrator trays, leaving space between pieces where possible.
Dehydrate at 125–135°F until firm and break apart with a snap — approximately 4–5 hours for thin slices, 7–8 hours for thicker pieces. Shreds may take only a few hours.
Cool completely and store in airtight containers in a cool, dry, dark place.
notes: Dehydrated potatoes rehydrate with a much closer taste and texture to fresh potatoes than canned. Sliced potatoes are great for scalloped potatoes or frying; diced for hash or soups; shredded for hashbrowns. Source: stockingmypantry.com

title: Easy All-Butter Flaky Pie Crust
ingredients: 1 teaspoon kosher salt (or 1/2 tsp fine sea salt)
1 cup (230g) very cold unsalted butter, cut into 1/2-inch cubes (2 sticks)
instructions: Add 1 1/2 cups flour, salt, and sugar (optional) to a food processor. Pulse 2–3 times until combined.
Scatter butter cubes over flour and process until a dough or paste begins to form, about 15 seconds. There should be no uncoated flour.
Scrape bowl, redistribute the flour-butter mixture, then add remaining 1 cup of flour. Pulse 4–5 times until flour is evenly distributed. Dough should look broken up and a little crumbly.

Transfer to a medium bowl, then sprinkle ice water over mixture — start with 4 tablespoons and add more as needed. Press the dough into itself with a rubber spatula until crumbs form larger clusters. If dough holds together when pinched, it's ready.
Place dough on a clean surface and work just enough to form a ball. Cut in half, form into discs, wrap in plastic wrap, and refrigerate at least 1 hour (up to 2 days) or freeze up to 3 months.
Add 1 1/2 cups flour, salt, and sugar (optional) to a medium bowl. Stir to combine. Scatter butter cubes over flour and mix briefly with a fork to coat.
Cut the butter into the flour with a pastry blender until the mixture has a coarse, mealy texture like fresh bread crumbs, about 1–2 minutes.

Add remaining 1 cup flour and work until evenly distributed, about 20 seconds. Sprinkle with 4–8 tablespoons ice water and press into itself until dough holds together. Form into discs, wrap, and refrigerate at least 1 hour.

Remove one disc from fridge and let sit 5 minutes. On a lightly floured surface, roll out to a 12-inch circle (about 1/8-inch thick). Transfer to pie dish by rolling onto the rolling pin and unrolling over the dish.
Press dough into the dish, trim to 1/2-inch overhang, fold edge under itself, and crimp. Refrigerate at least 20 minutes before baking.

For blind baking: Heat oven to 425°F with a baking sheet on the middle rack. Pierce crust with a fork, line with foil, fill with pie weights or dry beans, refrigerate 30 minutes. Bake 20–30 minutes at 400°F until golden. Brush with egg wash (1 yolk + 1 Tbsp cream) and bake 3–5 more minutes.
notes: For a double crust pie: do not crimp edges until after topping with second crust. Trim both crusts to 3/4-inch, fold top under bottom, then crimp. Brush with egg wash and sprinkle with 1 Tbsp sugar. Cut 3–4 slits in top. Source: inspiredtaste.net

title: Ham and Swiss Chicken with Mushrooms and Tomatoes

title: Homemade Flour Tortillas
ingredients: ⅓ cup extra virgin olive oil, vegetable oil, or other neutral-flavored oil
instructions: Combine flour, salt and baking powder in the bowl of a stand mixer. Using the dough hook, mix dry ingredients until well combined.

Add oil and water with mixer running at medium speed. After about 1 minute, or when mixture comes together and begins to form a ball, decrease mixing speed to low. Continue to mix for 1 minute or until dough is smooth.

Transfer dough to a lightly floured work surface. Divide into 16 equal portions. Turn each piece to coat with flour. Form each piece into a ball and flatten with the palm of your hand. Cover flattened balls with a clean kitchen towel and allow to rest for at least 15 minutes (or up to 2 hours) before proceeding.

After the rest period, heat a large pan over medium heat. Roll each dough piece into a rough circle, about 6–7 inches in diameter, keeping work surface and rolling pin lightly floured. Don't stack uncooked tortillas — separate with parchment paper.

When the pan is hot, place one dough circle into the pan and cook 45 seconds to 1 minute, until the bottom has a few pale brown spots and the top is bubbly. Adjust heat as needed. Flip and cook for 15–20 seconds. Tortillas should be soft with a few small brown spots.
Remove from pan with tongs and stack in a covered container or zippered bag to keep soft.

Serve warm or allow to cool. To reheat, place a slightly damp paper towel in the bottom of a covered microwave-safe container, add tortillas, and microwave uncovered for 15–30 seconds. Keep covered while serving.
Store in an airtight container at room temperature for 24 hours, or refrigerate for up to 1 week. To freeze, separate tortillas with parchment or waxed paper and place in a zippered bag.
notes: Makes 16 tortillas. Recipe source: thecafesucrefarine.com

title: Lasagna Stew
instructions: Select sauté mode and brown ground beef. Add onion, garlic, salt, pepper, and Italian seasoning. Once nearly browned, hit cancel.
Add remaining ingredients — spinach, zucchini, marinara, tomatoes, and chicken broth. Replace the lid and move valve to sealed position.

Heat a large stock pot to medium-high heat. Brown the meat with garlic and onion. Drain. Add salt, Italian seasoning and heat another minute. Add remaining ingredients. Turn heat to low and simmer 20 minutes.

title: Le Houllier Family Dressing
instructions: 1 Day or 1 Week in Advance: Place the bread in oven with oven light on. Place the bread in a single layer to dry out.
In an iron skillet brown hamburger and season with salt and pepper. When browned, remove with a slotted spoon leaving the fat in the skillet. Set aside to cool.
In the same skillet add the giblets, liver, onion, salt and pepper. Cook on medium high for 10–12 minutes stirring often. Cool for 30 minutes.
Remove from heat and add ½ cup of water, scraping the bottom of the pan.
In a food processor add giblet mixture and pulse until giblets are medium fine.
In a large bowl combine the giblet mixture and the hamburger. Set aside.

In a large bowl filled half full with warm water, dip the dry bread (4 slices at a time) and squeeze with hands until all water is removed. Place bread into the bowl with the meat mixture. Continue until all bread has been moistened and added.
Stir together with your hands, taste and adjust seasonings if needed. Refrigerate until needed.
When ready to cook, add the chicken broth and place in an oven-proof container. Cook for about 30 minutes. If cooking with a turkey, add turkey drippings to the dressing, stir and taste often.
notes: Le Houllier is pronounced "La-who-yer." It is French. Nana Rose has always made this dressing every year for the last 70+ years. This dressing has been handed down from the LeHoullier family for generations. Nana is not sure how old this recipe is, but she remembers her mother, Frances Le Houllier Creely, teaching her to make it. Nana used to grind the giblets raw with her grinder; in this recipe we cook the giblets first then use the food processor — there is no difference in taste. Nana Rose made this for Thanksgiving, Christmas, and Easter, and would also freeze it to serve smaller portions with dinners throughout the year.This dressing is a pain in the ass to make, but the holidays wouldn't be the same without it.

title: Lemon Buttercream Frosting
ingredients: ¼ cup (60g) milk, or more to reach desired consistency
Yellow Coloring Gel — optional (Americolor Lemon Yellow)
instructions: In the bowl of your mixer beat the butter until smooth. Blend in the lemon extract and zest.
Add half of the powdered sugar and half of the milk. Mix at medium speed until combined.
Add remaining powdered sugar and gradually add the remaining milk. Add a touch of lemon yellow coloring gel at this point if desired.
Mix at medium speed 3–5 minutes, scraping the sides and bottom of the bowl occasionally. At the end, slow mixer to very low speed for 1–2 minutes to eliminate air pockets.
notes: Yield: 6 cups — enough to fill and frost a 3-layer 8-inch cake. Can be frozen in an airtight container for at least 3 months; thaw on countertop and remix. If buttercream is too thin, add more powdered sugar. If too thick, add more milk 1 teaspoon at a time. Source: mycakeschool.com

title: Lemon Cake
instructions: Preheat the oven to 350°F. Grease and flour two 8-inch pans (2 inches deep). You can also divide between three 8-inch pans for thinner layers.
In a medium bowl, whisk together the flour, baking powder, baking soda, salt, and zest of 2 lemons. Set aside.
In another bowl, whisk together the milk, vegetable oil, lemon juice, and lemon extract. Set aside.
In the bowl of your mixer, beat the softened butter until smooth. Gradually add the sugar and mix on medium speed 3–5 minutes until lightened in color and fluffy.
Add the eggs one at a time, mixing until the yellow of the yolk disappears.
Add the flour mixture and the milk mixture alternately — begin and end with the flour (3 additions of flour, 2 of milk). Mix until combined and smooth. Do not overmix.

Pour the batter into the prepared pans, smoothing the tops. Bake at 350°F: three 8-inch pans for 20–25 minutes; two 8-inch pans for 30–35 minutes, until a toothpick comes out clean or with a few crumbs.
Let the cakes cool in the pans 10 minutes then turn out.
notes: No cake flour? For each cup of all-purpose flour, remove 2 Tbsp and replace with 2 Tbsp cornstarch. For this recipe: measure 3 cups all-purpose, remove 6 Tbsp, replace with 6 Tbsp cornstarch. Whisk to blend. Source: mycakeschool.com

title: Lemon Cream Cheese Buttercream
ingredients: 2 sticks (226g) unsalted butter, slightly softened but still cool to the touch
2 × 8oz packages full-fat cream cheese (453g total) — do not use reduced fat or tub containers — very slightly softened
1 teaspoon (4g) lemon extract — optional, for stronger lemon flavor
6 to 6½ cups (690g–747g) powdered sugar, adding more if necessary
instructions: Cut butter into ½-inch slices and add to the bowl of your mixer. Beat until smooth.
Cut the cream cheese into pieces and add to the butter, beating until blended.
Add the lemon juice, lemon zest, and optional lemon extract.
Gradually add powdered sugar and beat until well blended.
This frosting will pipe best if used while still chilled. Make in advance, refrigerate, and when ready to use let it soften slightly and remix. Do not microwave to soften.
notes: Will frost a 3-layer 8 or 9-inch cake. Source: mycakeschool.com

title: Lemon Curd
instructions: Combine sugar, cornstarch, and water in a saucepan over medium heat. Stir constantly until mixture thickens and comes to a boil (3–4 minutes).
Boil, stirring, for 1 minute more. Remove the pan from the heat.
Spoon about ½ cup of the hot mixture into a small bowl with the egg yolks and stir quickly until combined. Continue stirring while pouring the egg mixture back into the saucepan.
Return pan to medium heat and cook, stirring, until the curd is thickened and lemon-colored (1–2 minutes). Remove from heat and stir in the butter, lemon juice, and lemon zest.
Let the lemon curd cool, then press plastic wrap onto the surface to prevent a skin from forming. Refrigerate.
notes: Yield: 1¼ cups — will fill a 2–3 layer 8 or 9-inch cake with some leftover. Recipe can be halved. Can be kept refrigerated for 1 week. Source: mycakeschool.com

title: Lemon Orange Layer Cake
ingredients: ¾ cup (180g) fresh orange juice (juice of about 3 oranges)
3–4 Tablespoons (48g) lemon juice (juice of one lemon)
3 sticks (339g) unsalted butter, softened (should still feel cool and hold its shape, yet dent when pressed)
21 oz (678g) cream cheese, slightly softened — 3 × 8oz packages
9 cups (1,035g) confectioners' sugar, adjust to your liking
instructions: Preheat oven to 350°F. Grease and flour three 8-inch pans.
In a medium bowl, whisk together flour, baking powder, baking soda, salt, and zest of 2 lemons. Set aside.
In another bowl, whisk together milk, vegetable oil, lemon juice, and lemon extract. Set aside.
Beat softened butter until smooth. Gradually add sugar and mix on medium speed 3–5 minutes until lightened and fluffy.
Add eggs one at a time, mixing until yolk disappears. Add flour and milk mixtures alternately (begin and end with flour — 3 flour additions, 2 milk). Mix until smooth; do not overmix.
Pour into prepared pans. Bake at 350°F for 20–25 minutes until toothpick comes out clean. Cool in pans 10 minutes then turn out.
Zest all oranges and the lemon using a microplane, getting only the outer zest, not the white pith.
In a saucepan combine sugar, cornstarch, egg yolks, orange and lemon juice and zest, and butter. Stir to blend.
Cook on medium-low heat stirring until smooth, then increase to medium and stir constantly until mixture thickens and bubbles. Reduce heat and stir 1 minute more (pudding-like consistency).
Pour curd into a heatproof bowl. Cool 15 minutes, press plastic wrap onto the surface. Refrigerate until cool. The curd will thicken further as it cools.
Cut butter into slices, add to mixer bowl. Beat on low to medium speed until softened and smooth.
Cut cream cheese into pieces and add to butter, beating at low to medium speed until incorporated.
Add vanilla and orange flavorings. Gradually add powdered sugar on low speed until blended. Increase speed and beat until fluffy. Do not over beat.
To assemble: pipe a dam of orange cream cheese frosting ¼ inch from the edge of each cake layer, fill with a layer of curd, top with cream cheese frosting. Repeat, then frost the outside as usual.
notes: No cake flour? For each cup of all-purpose flour, remove 2 Tbsp and replace with 2 Tbsp cornstarch. For this recipe: measure 3 cups AP flour, remove 6 Tbsp, replace with 6 Tbsp cornstarch. Whisk to blend.If orange cream cheese frosting is too soft, add more powdered sugar or chill as needed. This frosting pipes best when still chilled — make in advance, refrigerate, soften slightly and remix (do not microwave). Source: mycakeschool.com

title: Lemon Pound Cake
ingredients: 1 cup (2 sticks) butter, at room temperature
1 cup sour cream, at room temperature
For the glaze: 1½ cups powdered sugar
For the glaze: 2–3 tablespoons freshly-squeezed lemon juice
instructions: Arrange a rack in the center position of the oven and preheat to 325°F. Generously grease and flour a 12-cup bundt pan and set aside.
In a large bowl, whisk together the flour, baking soda, salt, and lemon zest.
In another large bowl, use an electric mixer to cream together the butter and sugar. Increase speed to medium-high and beat about 5 minutes until light and fluffy.
Add the eggs one at a time, mixing well after each addition.

With the mixer on low speed, alternately add half the flour mixture, half the sour cream, then the remaining flour, sour cream, lemon juice, and lemon extract. Mix until just combined — do not overmix.
Transfer batter to prepared pan. Smooth the top with a spatula and firmly tap the pan on the counter to release air bubbles.
Bake for 1 hour 15 minutes to 1 hour 30 minutes, until a few moist crumbs stick to a toothpick inserted in the center.
Cool cake in the pan for 15 minutes before turning out onto a wire rack to finish cooling completely.

For the glaze: measure powdered sugar into a bowl and stir in 2 tablespoons lemon juice until completely smooth. Add more lemon juice if glaze is too thick. Drizzle over the cooled cake and allow to harden. Garnish with additional lemon zest if desired.
notes: Source: fivehearthome.com

title: Lemon Whipped Cream Filling
instructions: Combine sugar, cornstarch and water in a saucepan over medium heat. Stir constantly until mixture thickens and comes to a boil (3–4 minutes). Boil, stirring, for 1 minute more. Remove the pan from the heat.
Spoon about 1/2 cup of the hot mixture into a small bowl with egg yolks and stir quickly until combined. Continue stirring while pouring the egg mixture back into the saucepan.
Return pan to medium heat and cook, stirring, until the curd is thickened and lemon colored (1–2 minutes).
Remove from heat and stir in the butter, lemon juice, and lemon zest.
Let the lemon curd cool, then press plastic wrap onto the surface to prevent a skin from forming. Refrigerate.
Chill the mixing bowl and beaters (for hand mixer) or whisk attachment (for stand mixer) in the freezer for 10 minutes.
Pour the whipping cream, powdered sugar and vanilla into the chilled mixing bowl, begin beating on low speed gradually increasing to medium high speed.
Beat until the cream begins to thicken (after approx. 1½ minutes) and soft peaks form — peaks that slightly bend over. Watch closely as this happens quickly.
Gently fold 1/2 cup (134g) chilled lemon curd into the prepared whipped cream.
You will have leftover lemon curd which you can use to glaze your cake layers before spreading with lemon whipped cream filling.
notes: Recipe from My Cake School.

title: Low Carb Dijon Shrimp Skillet
ingredients: 10 oz steamer bag cut green beans

instructions: Place frozen green beans in microwave and cook per package instructions.

Meanwhile, run shrimp under cold water to thaw. Remove tails and set aside. Rinse sun-dried tomatoes under water then chop. Tip: place them in a small bowl and use kitchen shears to chop them quickly.
Heat a large skillet to medium-high heat. Add sun-dried tomatoes. Remove green beans from steamer bag and add to skillet. Cook for 2 minutes.
Add shrimp to skillet. Then add remaining ingredients, stirring until well incorporated. Turn heat to low simmer and cook 5 minutes more.
Remove from heat and serve.

title: Lumpia
ingredients: 1 bag coleslaw (only need the veggies — carrots & cabbage)
30 lumpia wrappers (spring roll wrappers will work — purchase at Global Foods in Kirkwood)
instructions: Heat 1 tablespoon vegetable oil in a wok or large skillet over high heat. Add pork; cook and stir until crumbly and no pink is showing, 5 to 7 minutes.
Remove pork from the pan and set aside. Drain grease from the pan, leaving just a thin coating.
Add onion and garlic to the pan; cook and stir until fragrant, about 2 minutes. Stir in the cooked pork, carrots, green onions and cabbage.
Season with pepper, salt, garlic powder, and soy sauce. Remove from the heat, and set aside until cool enough to handle, about 5 minutes.

Assemble lumpia: Place 3 heaping tablespoons of filling diagonally near one corner of a lumpia wrapper, leaving a 1½ inch space at both ends. Fold like a small burrito. Moisten the other side of the wrapper with water to seal the edge.
Transfer to a plate and cover with plastic wrap to retain moisture. Repeat to assemble remaining lumpia.
Brush lumpia rolls with a little olive oil on all sides.
Bake in air fryer for 10 minutes at 400°. Turn halfway through. Cook until golden brown on all sides.
notes: A Filipino dish we started making for Carter in 2020.

title: Mocha Cake
instructions: Preheat oven to 350°. Line three 9-inch cake pans with parchment paper.
In a large bowl, cream butter and brown sugar until light and fluffy. Add eggs, one at a time, beating well after each addition. Beat in vanilla.
In another bowl, whisk flour, cocoa, baking soda, and salt; add to creamed mixture alternately with sour cream, beating well after each addition. Carefully stir in coffee until blended.
Evenly pour cake batter between each cake pan. Bake for 30–35 minutes or until a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean.
Cool in pans 10 minutes before removing to wire racks to cool completely.
In a large microwave-safe bowl, add butter and chocolates. Microwave in 15-second intervals, stirring between each, until smooth. Cool slightly.
Add confectioners' sugar, sour cream, salt, and vanilla to the chocolate mixture and beat until smooth.
Spread frosting between layers and over top and sides of cake. Refrigerate leftovers.
notes: You can bake the cake in a 9-by-13 cake pan as well. You can substitute hot coffee for hot water for an espresso-chocolate cake! Recipe from Scratch Pantry.

title: Mom's Easy Refrigerator Rolls
ingredients: Flour (enough to make a medium sticky dough)
instructions: Dissolve 1 pkg of yeast in a cup of warm water.
In a separate bowl, combine boiling water, butter and sugar. Stir until butter has melted.
Add cold water to the butter & sugar mixture. Then add yeast mixture and egg.
Add enough flour to make a medium sticky dough. Omit salt if using self-rising flour.
Cover with plastic wrap and put in refrigerator to raise overnight.
Melt butter in a small dish. Preheat oven to 425°.
To make rolls, pinch off a small piece of dough.
Dip in melted butter and fold in half and place in a 9x13 cake pan with sides.
Let rise until doubled in size or near the top edge of the pan.
Bake until golden brown.
notes: Patty Davidson's recipe. Make-Ahead / Par-Bake Option: After the Day 1 rise, shape rolls and place in the 9x13 dish without baking. Refrigerate overnight to rise. Bake at 200° for 1–1¼ hours until cooked through but not browned (par-baked). Cool on a wire rack. Freeze individually or refrigerate 1–3 days. To brown and serve: preheat oven to 350° and bake 15 minutes until golden brown. For best results, let frozen rolls thaw before baking.

title: No Knead Artisan Bread
ingredients: 3 cups (360–390g / 12¾ oz) all-purpose or bread flour, plus about 2 Tbsp extra for shaping
1/4 teaspoon active dry or instant yeast (1g)
1½ cups hot water, not boiling (354 mL)
instructions: Combine flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Stir in water until well combined.
Cover with plastic wrap and let stand at room temperature for 3 hours.

After 3 hours dough will be puffy and dotted with bubbles. Transfer to a well-floured surface and sprinkle with a little flour. Using a scraper, fold dough over 10–12 times and shape into a rough ball.
Place in a parchment-paper-lined bowl (not wax paper) and cover with a towel. Let stand on the countertop for about 35 minutes.
Meanwhile, place a 5-qt Dutch oven with lid in a cold oven and preheat to 450°F.
When oven reaches 450°F, carefully lift the parchment paper and dough from the bowl and place gently into the hot pot (parchment paper goes in too). Cover and bake for 30 minutes.
After 30 minutes, remove lid and parchment paper. Return uncovered to oven and bake 10–15 more minutes. Let cool at least 15 minutes before slicing.
notes: Notes from Jenny: The standard for weighing flour is 1 cup = 120g, but when measured one cup can come to 125–130g. Use what works for you. Recipe from jennycancook.com.

title: Old Fashioned Cocoa Cake
instructions: Preheat oven to 350°.
Grease (2) 8" cake pans with butter then dust with cocoa powder.
In a separate bowl, combine flour, salt, baking soda and baking powder. Stir to blend.
In mixing bowl, cream butter, sugar and eggs at high speed until well blended.
Alternate adding flour mix and water to mixing bowl. Blend until well combined.
Divide the batter into the 2 cake pans. Bake for 30-35 minutes.
Let cool 10 minutes before removing cakes from the pans. Wrap each cake individually in plastic wrap and cool in the refrigerator.
Thoroughly combine dry ingredients in a 4 qt sauce pan over medium heat. Stir in milk.
Bring to a boil, stirring constantly.

Reduce heat slightly. Stir constantly until the mixture reaches the soft ball stage or 236° on a candy thermometer. (Soft ball stage: If you don't have a candy thermometer, boil for about 5 minutes then drop a small amount into COLD water — if you can form it into a soft ball, it's done.)
Remove from heat. Add butter and vanilla. DO NOT STIR!!!!
Cool at room temperature or 110° on candy thermometer.
Beat until frosting thickens and loses its gloss. Frost the cake. If it is too thick, add small amounts of milk and beat until spreading is easy.
notes: Mamaw Call's recipe.

title: Sarku Japan Teriyaki Chicken
ingredients: 2 carrots, shredded (or use a veggie peeler)
1 cup cooked rice (cooked the day before is best)
instructions: Shred cabbage and carrots. Add to wok (or chicken fryer) with a little sesame oil. Cook until cabbage is tender.
While cabbage is cooking, dice the chicken and sauté in oil. Season with salt and pepper.
In a sauce pan, combine all teriyaki sauce ingredients (not the thickening ingredients yet). Bring to a light boil, stirring until sugar is dissolved.
In a small dish, mix cornstarch and cold water until smooth. Add to sauce and bring to a light boil again, stirring until sauce is thick and coats a spoon well.
When cabbage is tender, add cooked rice and heat until warm. Add a little water to the pan if needed to keep rice from sticking.
To serve: Add cabbage/carrot/rice mix to a bowl, top with chicken. Pour a little teriyaki sauce over the top.
notes: To make this even faster, you can use a bag of coleslaw mix (cabbage and carrots only — no dressing) in place of the cabbage and carrots in the recipe.

title: Sharon's Cocoa Cake
instructions: Preheat oven to 350°. Grease 2 round 9-inch baking pans with butter. Dust with cocoa powder.
Cream butter, sugar and eggs until fluffy. Beat on high speed for 3 minutes, then reduce to low speed until fluffy.
Combine flour, cocoa, baking soda, baking powder and salt. Add alternating with milk into the creamed mixture.
Pour equal amounts into baking pans.
Bake at 350° for 35 minutes. Check at 30 minutes. Cake is done when a toothpick stuck into the center comes out clean.
Frosting: Melt butter in a sauce pan over medium heat. Add cocoa; heat for 1 minute or until smooth, stirring constantly. Pour into mixer bowl.
Alternately add confectioner's sugar and milk. Beat to spreading consistency. Blend in vanilla. Add more sugar for desired thickness.
notes: Sharon's version of Mamaw Call's Cocoa Cake Recipe.

title: Slow-Cooker Rustic French Onion Soup
instructions: Spray 6- to 7-quart slow cooker with cooking spray. In slow cooker, place onions, butter, salt, pepper and dried thyme. In medium bowl, mix flour, sherry and water with wire whisk until blended. Stir into onion mixture.
Cover; cook on Low heat setting 9 to 10 hours or until onions are softened and deep brown.
Stir broth into slow cooker. Increase heat setting to High. Cover; cook 10 minutes or until hot.

Meanwhile, set oven control to broil. Line cookie sheet with foil. Arrange baguette slices in single layer on cookie sheet. Broil with tops 3 inches from heat 2 minutes, turning once, until lightly browned.
Sprinkle cheese evenly over slices; broil 1 minute longer or until cheese is bubbly.
Top each bowl of soup with 2 cheese toasts. Sprinkle with fresh thyme.
notes: Recipe source: Betty Crocker — Prep Time: 35 min | Cook Time: 9 hr 35 min | Servings: 10

instructions: In a mixing bowl add all meatball ingredients. Mixture will be messy and sticky. Use hands to divide into meatballs on a lined baking sheet. Will make approximately 9 large meatballs.
Bake for 12–15 minutes until internal temperature reaches 160°F. Use the back of a spoon to remove any rendered fat. Place on a plate lined in paper towels and blot any seen oil.
While meatballs cook, make zucchini noodles. Put in a microwave safe bowl with a vented lid and microwave for 4 minutes, or heat in a small saucepan until cooked. No extra liquid is needed.
Add marinara sauce to zucchini noodles. The cooked noodles will warm the sauce — if you want it warmer, return to microwave or heat over stove.
Serve meatballs with marinara zucchini noodles.
notes: One meatball will be approximately 1.3 ounces after cooking. Serving size for calculation purposes is 2.25 meatballs (just under 3 ounces).

title: Turkey Sausage Bell Pepper Egg Cups
filename: Turkey Sausage Bell Pepper Egg Cups.html
instructions: Preheat oven to 350°F.
Whisk eggs together. Stir in cottage cheese. Add bell peppers, turkey sausage and cheddar cheese.
Spray a muffin pan generously with cooking spray. Pour in egg mixture — about 3/4 of the way full.
Bake for 30 minutes. Remove from muffin pan immediately, then allow to cool 3–4 minutes before serving.
notes: Serving sizes on the recipes are not necessarily "bariatric sized" portions but are "traditional" portion sizes.

title: Vegetable Broth Powder
instructions: Select a variety of dehydrated vegetables to use in your powdered vegetable stock. Onions, celery, carrots, and peas are some of the most popular powdered vegetable broth ingredients, but you can use your imagination here. Add in any veggies you love!
Mix all ingredients together and pulverize them to a powder using a blender, food processor, coffee grinder, or spice grinder.

Season to your taste and dietary needs. Add salt, if you wish, and a blend of your own favorite dried herbs and spices to the powdered vegetables. A few favorites include black pepper, basil, thyme, paprika, nutritional yeast, celery seed, and marjoram.
Store your vegetable powder in an airtight container in a dark, cool, dry place. If stored correctly, your vegetable broth powder will keep for months without refrigeration.
notes: Reconstitute with water to make a quick vegetarian stock — add 1 tablespoon of vegetable powder for every cup of boiling water. Add directly to cooking water for grains like rice, quinoa, or polenta for easy flavor. Use as a seasoning sprinkle (great on popcorn!). Blend into cream cheese for a vegetable spread. To make vegetarian gravy: add 2 tablespoons to 1 cup of water, simmer to reduce, then thicken with 1 tablespoon cornstarch mixed with 2 tablespoons cold water.
